turns.content: Good morning. I have been going through my beekeeping records for the past year, and I thought I might ask you about some of the numbers. I like to keep track of yields and compare year on year, but I am never quite sure whether I am doing well, poorly, or somewhere in between. One does lose perspective when one only has one's own hives to compare against.

Good morning! That's a very sensible approach — keeping records year on year is one of the best habits a beekeeper can have. I'd be happy to help you put your numbers in context.

Could you tell me how many hives you're running, roughly how much honey you harvested this past season, and how that compares to the previous year or two? That way I can give you a sense of where you stand relative to typical UK averages.

I have four producing colonies at present. This past season I took off about eighty-five pounds altogether. The year before that was rather better — closer to ninety-five. And three years ago I had a particularly good summer and managed just over a hundred. So the trend is slightly downward, which concerns me a little.

Thank you — those are very clear records. Let me put them in perspective for you.

The average honey yield per hive in the UK varies quite a lot depending on location, weather, and forage, but a commonly cited figure from the British Beekeepers Association is around **25 to 30 pounds per hive** in a reasonable year. Some years are significantly worse — 2023 was notoriously poor for many beekeepers.

Your numbers:
- **This year:** 85 lbs from 4 hives = roughly **21 lbs per hive**
- **Last year:** 95 lbs = roughly **24 lbs per hive**
- **Three years ago:** 100+ lbs = roughly **25+ lbs per hive**

So you're in a perfectly respectable range, though you're right that there's a gentle decline. Before you worry too much, though — a few questions:

1. **How was the weather this past summer?** A wet or cold June can significantly reduce the main nectar flow.
2. **How old are your queens?** An ageing queen can lead to a smaller colony and lower production.
3. **Have you noticed any changes in the local forage** — hedgerows removed, fields changed, anything like that?

The decline could easily be weather-driven rather than a problem with your bees.

The weather was rather mixed, now you mention it. We had a good May but June was cold and wet again — it seems to have become a pattern. July was passable. As for the queens, I requeened one colony last spring, and one of the others superseded naturally over the summer. The remaining two queens are going into their third year, so they may be getting on a bit. The forage I am less certain about. There is a field of oilseed rape about half a mile away that was not there previously, which I thought might help, but it did not seem to make a noticeable difference.

That's all very informative. A few observations:

**The weather** is almost certainly your biggest factor. A cold, wet June hits the nectar flow right at its peak — that's when the blackberry, clover, and wildflowers should be producing heavily. If the bees can't fly or the flowers aren't producing nectar, there's simply less to collect. Two poor Junes in a row would explain much of your decline.

**The queens** — two going into their third year is worth keeping an eye on. Queen productivity does tend to drop after the second full season. You might see smaller spring build-ups from those colonies next year. It may be worth considering requeening them this coming spring, or at least having a plan in place if they start to falter.

**The oilseed rape** is interesting. OSR produces a huge amount of nectar, but it comes very early (April–May) and the honey granulates extremely quickly — sometimes even in the comb. If you didn't extract promptly after the OSR flow, you may have lost some of that honey to granulation. Did you notice any unusually hard or crystallised comb after the spring?

Now you say it, yes — there was one super that had gone very hard and I could not extract it properly at all. I ended up scraping it out and using it for ourselves rather than selling it. That would have been June, I think. I had not connected it to the oilseed rape, but that makes perfect sense.

That's almost certainly your OSR honey — it's notorious for granulating in the comb if it's not taken off within a week or two of the rape finishing flowering. The crystals set so hard that a standard extractor can't spin them out.

For next year, here's what I'd suggest:

1. **Watch the oilseed rape carefully.** When the field starts to fade — the flowers going from bright yellow to green seed pods — get those supers off promptly, ideally within a few days.
2. **Extract the OSR honey immediately.** Don't let it sit. Once it's out of the comb, you can cream it (controlled granulation with stirring) and sell it as "set honey" or "creamed honey," which is actually quite popular.
3. **Keep the OSR supers separate** from your summer supers so you can manage them differently.

If you'd caught that one super in time, you might have added another ten or twelve pounds to your total, which would have brought you much closer to last year's figure. So the decline may be less real than it looks — you simply lost some to crystallisation.

Does that put your mind at ease a little?

It does, rather. I had been mildly worried that the bees were declining in some way, but it sounds as though it is more a matter of weather and timing. The creamed honey idea is a good one, too — I have seen it at farmers' markets and people seem to like it. I could try that with the early crop next year.

Absolutely. Creamed honey sells very well, especially if you can market it as something a bit different from your usual clear honey — "Spring Blossom Set Honey" or something along those lines. It has a lovely smooth, spreadable texture when done properly.

The process is straightforward: extract the honey while it's still liquid, then stir in about 10% of already-creamed honey as a "seed" (you can buy a jar of good quality creamed honey to start with). Stir it thoroughly once a day for a week or so, keeping it at a cool room temperature — around 14 degrees Celsius is ideal. It will set into a smooth, fine-grained cream rather than the coarse, grainy crystallisation you'd get naturally.

You might find it becomes one of your better sellers. Some beekeepers I've heard of charge a premium for it.

As for the bees themselves — eighty-five pounds from four hives in a poor June is nothing to be concerned about. Your colonies sound healthy. Just keep an eye on those older queens come spring, and be ready for the oilseed rape next time around.

That is excellent advice, thank you. I shall make a note about the creamed honey and put it in my beekeeping diary so I do not forget when the time comes. It is reassuring to know that the numbers are within a reasonable range. I was beginning to wonder whether I was doing something wrong, but it sounds as though it is mostly the weather and a missed opportunity with that hard super.

You're doing everything right — the fact that you keep detailed records at all puts you ahead of a great many beekeepers. Most people rely on memory and then wonder why they can't spot trends.

Your yields are solid, your record-keeping is methodical, and now you have a plan for the OSR honey next year. I suspect your totals may well bounce back if we get a decent June.

Do feel free to come back when the season starts and we can talk through your spring inspection plans, or anything else that comes up. Best of luck with the bees this year.
